title: Dance event for Colored People in 1929
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
White paper poster. “DANCE! COLORED FOLKS AT FORUM ROOF, Waterloo’s most beautiful ball room has been given over for one night to you for your entertainment and pleasure, on JULY 24, 1929. . . . Let’s all be there and have a lot of good clean fun as we have before,” printed in red ink at poster’s anterior. 

Donated to the museum by Mrs. Sylvester B. Howell following the death of her husband, manager of the Howell Printing Company in Waterloo, in 1971.  

Forum Roof was a dance hall located at the corner of East 11th Street and Sycamore Street in Waterloo.  This poster is strong evidence of segregation in the absence of Jim Crow Laws.  
The date of July 24, 1929 (a Wednesday) would place this event in close temporal proximity to Emancipation Day, celebrated in early August.
subject: African Americans; Black people; Dance; African American dance; Visual communication; Enslaved persons--Emancipation; "Enslaved persons--Emancipation--Anniversaries; etc."

title: Emancipation Celebration Poster from the 1920s
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
White paper poster. “EMANCIPATION CELEBRATION, TUESDAY, AUG. 6, PICNIC AND BARBECUE, AUSPICES, National Association for the Advancement of Colored People. . . . ELECTRIC PARK – WATERLOO, IA.” printed in black ink at poster’s anterior.  

 

Donated to the museum by Mrs. Sylvester B. Howell following the death of her husband, manager of the Howell Printing Company in Waterloo, in 1971. 

 

Based on similarities to other posters printed by Howell, we may date this poster to the late 1920s through about 1935.     

 

Emancipation Day was an American holiday celebrated by Blacks in commemoration of the abolition of slavery in the British West Indies on 1 August 1834.  The first Emancipation Day celebration in Iowa was held in Muscatine in 1857.  The holiday came to Waterloo in the 1910s, following significant black migration to the city.  The NAACP negotiated annually with the ownership of Electric Park, an amusement park adjacent to the National Dairy Cattle Congress grounds, to reserve the park for an entire day.  In addition to the regularly-offered amusement park entertainment, there was usually a parade, speakers, and dancing at the Electric Park Ballroom.  Emancipation Day has been largely replaced by Juneteenth in Waterloo.
subject: African Americans; Black people; National Association for the Advancement of Colored People; "Enslaved persons--Emancipation--Anniversaries; etc."; Anniversaries; Inventions; Visual communication

title: Black Lives Matter Protest Poster 1
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
White cardstock sign. "Christ + racism DON’T MIX. YOU can’t say ‘I love God’ + hate His creation. #blacklivesmatter," handwritten in black ink at anterior.


One of three such posters taped to lightposts at the intersections of Washington and W Park and Washington and W 4th in Waterloo, Iowa, before the George Floyd protest march of the night of 6/2-6/3/2020. The March began at the Black Hawk Co. Jail and was ultimately broken up by the Waterloo Police Department in Washington Park and on the 218 overpass. Grout Museum District staff collected the signs after they blew down in a storm on 6/5/2020.

title: Black Lives Matter Protest Gas Gernade
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Top 1/3 of a “Triple-Chaser” gas grenade. When deployed, the grenade body breaks into three parts to distribute gas over a wider area. Body reads "CM" and "TO BE USED BY TRAINED LAW ENFORCEMENT, CORRECTIONAL OR MILITARY PERSONNEL" stamped in blue-green ink, indicating that the body was filled with CS gas.


Used by the Waterloo Police Department during the George Floyd protest march of the night of 6/2-6/3/2020. The March began at the Black Hawk Co. Jail and was ultimately broken up by the Waterloo Police Department in Washington Park and on the 218 overpass. Found by Grout Museum District personnel in the grass in front of the Carl A. & Peggy J. Bluedorn Science Imaginarium, between the sidewalk and the building facade on 6/4/2020.

title: Black Lives Matter Protest Poster 6
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Digital image (5472 x 3648). Snapshot of chalk writing in pink and blue on a red brick walkway, “OUR Daughters Matter.”


Image taken on 6/4/2020 by Grout Museum District personnel. One of several such writings made in Waterloo’s Lincoln Park during the George Floyd Protest of 5/29/2020. Protest march began at Waterloo Veterans Memorial Hall and ended at Lincoln Park with speeches and declarations. Park was still under renovations when the protest was held. Construction workers had to wash the chalk away before the paving could be sealed on 6/5/2020.

collectionid: Grout Museum District 2020 George Floyd Protests
title: Nia Wilder’s T-Shirt from George Floyd Protests
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Crew-necked short-sleeve t-shirt of red cotton/polyester blend. Machine-stitched.  Stylized letter “B” inside a lightbulb printed at center anterior.   

Graphic (Meant to be interpreted as the imperative: “Be light.”) is the personal brand of local businessowner Nia “ShinDigg” Wilder.  Pieces featuring the brand are commonly referred to as “Light Gear.” Wilder was a major organizer among Waterloo’s African-American community during the 2020 George Floyd Protests. Wilder sold/distributed “light gear” widely in 2020 and such pieces were commonly seen at local social justice events that year. Wilder was elected Ward 3 Waterloo City Councilperson in 2022.   

From donor Nia “ShinDigg” Wilder:

“Connecting to the Black Lives Matter Movement is more than just marching and protesting. To me, “Black Lives Matter” is ensuring that every Black Life understands how important they are and understands their role in making sure our community thrives. My mission with “Be Light” gear is to remind you WHO you are and WHY you are. Bringing Light into the community is my way of projecting something positive and owning that we all bare something in common, we all have a PURPOSE. BLACK LIVES MATTER!”

rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Button with safety pin clasp at posterior. Graphic at anterior printed on paper and crimped under transparent plastic covering. "BEAT THE Streetz" in white and yellow letters over a crossed-out revolver next to color representation of a street on a white background.

Beat the Streetz Foundation is a Waterloo nonprofit dedicated to providing education and resources to area youth, young adults, and adults for the prevention of gun violence. It’s founder, Raynelle Walker, is sister of local citizen Greg Walker, murdered 5/19/2018. The case remains unsolved. His nickname, “Streetz,” is the origin of the Foundation’s name.  
Beat the Streetz contributed to the organization of several Waterloo protests in 2020.

collectionid: Grout Museum District 2020 George Floyd Protest
title: Joyce Levingston’s Protest Kit from 2020 George Floyd Protests
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Drawstring backpack of pink nylon. Machine-stitched. Nylon cord makes shoulder straps and cinch closure. Backpack contains:

One pair safety glasses.
One Ziplock bag containing one pair nitrile gloves and one covid mask.
One Ziplock bag containing five band-aids and five prepackaged alcohol wipes.
Two bottled waters. 
Two chocolate chip granola bars.  
Two bags of mixed fruit gummi snacks.
One prepackaged rain poncho.
One bottle hand sanitizer. 
One tube sunscreen.  

Made and distributed to protestors by Organizer Joyce Levingston during the August 31 George Floyd Protest at Hawkeye Community College. Levingston borrowed the idea of protest kits from earlier protests in Ames and Des Moines. Levingston herself organized (with others) the May 29 march from Veterans Memorial Hall to Lincoln Park in Waterloo and the August 31 Hawkeye Community College Protest. 

From donor Joyce Levingston:
“When I heard George Floyd call out to his momma knowing he was going to lose his life, my heart shattered. It was at that moment that I knew in my lifetime I had seen this happen too much to my own. After hearing about Breonna Taylor and seeing what happened to George Floyd and many others in my lifetime I looked at my three daughters and my son and decided we need to come together in our community for all of our voices to be heard and mourn together for once instead of us carrying the weight on our own.  Rest in peace Breonna Taylor and rest in peace George Floyd.”
subject: African Americans; Black people; Black lives matter movement; Protest movements; African Americans--Violence against; Police brutality

collectionid: Grout Museum District 2020 George Floyd Protests
title: Vikki Brown’s Speech from 2020 George Floyd Protest
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Printed email (2 pages) sent from Vikki Brown to Nicholas Erickson on October 30, 2020.  Email contains speech delivered by Brown on Tuesday October 14th in front of the Waterloo Police Department.  

Vikki Brown is the Chairwoman for the Black Hawk County Democrats.  In 2020 she helped organize several of the perhaps one dozen protests held in Waterloo and Cedar Falls.  The October 14 protest was one of the last held that year.  It began with a march from Lincoln Park to the Waterloo Police Department, and concluded with speeches made in the parking lot.  Other Speakers included Waterloo Mayor Quentin Hart, Waterloo Chief of Police Dr. Joel Fitzgerald, Organizer Joyce Levingston, and Organizer Nia Wilder.

From donor Vikki Brown:

“The civil rights movement in the south was very unstable during the Jim Crow era of the 1950s and 1960s; and at times it was also violent. Growing up in Birmingham, Alabama during that era as a little girl, I felt compelled to participate on the right side of history as a Child Crusader. Fearing for our safety, my mother wouldn’t allow me nor my sisters to go near the chaos, but in 1963 at the age of 11 I sneaked down with classmates and marched against injustice. The ugliness hit close to home when a neighborhood acquaintance, also 11 years old, lost her life in the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing in September of that same year. Today, 57 years later I still continue with a vision on the right side of history. I have always, and I will continue to have a personal commitment to making a positive difference and impact in my community far and wide.”
subject: African Americans; Black people; Black lives matter movement; Protest movements; African Americans--Violence against; Police brutality

collectionid: Grout Museum District 2020 George Floyd Protests
title: George Floyd Protest Sign
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
White cardstock sign. "Black lives matter. Black children matter. Black love matters. BLACK FUTURES matter." handwritten in black ink at anterior. 

One of three such posters taped to lightposts at the intersections of Washington and W Park and Washington and W 4th in Waterloo, Iowa, before the George Floyd protest march of the night of 6/2-6/3/2020. The March began at the Black Hawk Co. Jail and was ultimately broken up by the Waterloo Police Department in Washington Park and on the 218 overpass. Grout Museum District staff collected the signs after they blew down in a storm on 6/5/2020.

collectionid: Grout Museum District African American Clubs/Groups/Organizations
title: Souvenir Program, MIDDLE WESTERN STATES ASS’N. IBPOE OF W, THIRTY-SECOND ANNUAL ASSOCIATION, JUNE 22 – 23 – 24 – 25, 1958, WATERLOO, IOWA
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Paperbound printed pamphlet.  Red paper cover.  Printed in black ink.  20 pages.  Contains names of and photos of the officers of the Temples, Lodges, and ancillary bodies of the Middle Western States Association of the Improved Benevolent and Protective Order of Elks of the World (IBPOE of W).   
  
The Improved Benevolent and Protective Order of Elks of the World was founded in 1898 in response to the Benevolent and Protective Order of Elks (BPOE) prohibition on Black membership.
In 1958, the Cedar Valley Lodge No. 426 and Melrose Temple No. 277 were located at 603 Shilliam Avenue in Waterloo.

title: GRANT P.T.A. YEAR BOOK, 1956, 1957
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Paperbound printed pamphlet.  Green paper cover.  Printed in black ink.  16 pages.  Contains listings of Grant Elementary Parent-Teacher Association meetings for academic year 1956/57 and its officers, as well as additional information about the values and functions of the PTA.     
  
Ulysses S. Grant Elementary School was opened in 1915 and located at the corner of Mobile and Cottage Streets in Waterloo’s “Northeast Triangle” or “North End,” the city’s most prominent historic Black neighborhood.  Before the Bridgeway Project integrated the school in 1970, Grant Elementary’s student body was 99% Black.

collectionid: Grout Museum District African American Politics Collection
title: Martha Nash Waterloo board of supervisors election handbill, 1982
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Printed in black ink on newsprint.  Folded once.  4 pages.  No pagination.  Contains information on Nash’s experience, credentials, and goals.     

Nash’s 1982 bid for election to the board of supervisors was unsuccessful.   
  
Martha Ann Furgerson Nash (September 26, 1925 – April 14, 2000), civil rights activist and community and religious leader, was the daughter of Dr. Lee B. and Lily Furgerson.  Served as director of the Black Hawk County Chapter of the NAACP throughout the 1960s. Executive director of the Martin Luther King, Jr. Center for Education and Vocational Training in Waterloo from 1981 until her retirement on February 1, 1995.

collectionid: Grout Museum District African American Politics Collection
title: William Parker municipal court judge election poster, 1963
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Black print on white cardstock.  “Wm (Bill) PARKER For Municipal Court Judge, Nov. 5, ELECTION.”  5½ x 3” card glued at upper right contains information on Parker’s credentials.

Judge William Parker was elected as the first black judge in Black Hawk County in 1963.  He is believed to be the second black judge elected in Iowa (Luther Glanton was elected municipal judge in Des Moines in 1959).  Parker left the position in 1971 and passed in 1973.

collectionid: Grout Museum District African American Politics Collection
title: ELECT Willie Mae Wright handbill for Waterloo City Council Fourth Ward, 1982
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Printed in blue ink on white paper.  Single sheet folded once.  4 pages.  No pagination.  Contains description of Ms. Wright’s credentials, accomplishments, and goals.  
Her bid for election was successful, and Ms. Wright became the second Black person to serve on the Waterloo City Council.    
  
Willie Mae Wright was born in Goodman, Mississippi in 1932 and moved to Waterloo in 1955.  She represented Waterloo’s Fourth and Sixth Wards on the City Council from 1982 to 1991.  She is an active community member, having served in leadership positions in the Waterloo Women’s Civic Club, the Black Hawk County NAACP, the Boy Scouts of America, School PTAs, The Jesse Cosby Neighborhood Center, the Black Hawk-Bremer League of Women Voters, and many others.

collectionid: Grout Museum District African American Politics Collection
title: “VOTE THE WHITIS TICKET” Waterloo municipal election poster, 1969
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Printed in black ink on white-faced heavy cardstock.  Depicts candidates of the “Whitis Ticket” for the 1969 municipal election in Waterloo.  Image of Ada Tredwell, candidate for East Side Councilman at Large, has been cut out and is missing.  
Handwritten election returns in blue ballpoint pen, presumably made by a Whitis Ticket staffer on election night, 1969, at reverse.  

This was the first all-black municipal election ticket in Waterloo history.  No candidate won election.  Lindbergh Whitis placed third for mayor with 1,139 votes.  

Lindbergh Whitis – July 2, 1928 – February 29, 2000.  Mayoral candidate, employed by John Deere and Rath Packing Co.  He also owned many businesses, including Lindbergh Asphalt Co., a limousine service, a lawn care service, the Joy Motel and a bail/bond service.  

Ada Treadwell – October 22, 1916 – August 30, 2008.  East Side Councilman at large candidate, employed by Rath Packing Co., John Deere Tractor Works, African Palace, later named the Martin Luther King Jr. Center.  Founder of the Eastside Citizens Committee.

James Andrerson Jr – June 21, 1931 – June 7, 2008.  Councilman Fourth Ward candidate.  Employed by Rath Packing Co., Waterloo Greyhound Park.  Was a member of the Waterloo Human Rights Commission, NAACP Freedom Dinner fund chairman, Waterloo Low Housing Commission, and Family Service League.

Tommie Henderson – June 27, 1920 – December 13, 1975. Riverfront Commissioner candidate.  Employed by Rath Packing Co., served in the army during WWII.  Was a charter member of Knights of Phythias, president of the East Side Citizens Committee, and past Post Commander of VFW 9130.

John McGee – August 23, 1932 – February 19, 2001.  Councilman Third Ward candidate.  Served in the U. S. Army during the Korean War.  Employed by John Deere Waterloo Tractor Works.  

Dr. Warren Nash – December 28, 1923 – March 27, 1979.  Park Commissioner candidate.  Married Martha Nash.  Served in the Navy during WWII.  Began practicing medicine in Waterloo August 1954. Was the only black physician at the time.  Was a member of the Black Hawk County and Iowa Medical Societies.  Was on the staff at Allen Memorial Hospital and the courtesy staffs at both St. Francis and Schoitz Memorial hospitals.  President of the Black Hawk County Branch of the NAACP for six years – 1962-1968.  Was on the board of the Afro-American Community Broadcasting Corp.
subject: African Americans; Black people; African American politicians; African Americans--Politics and government; African American women; African American women political activists

collectionid: Grout Museum District African American Newspapers
title: Waterloo Star, Volume 1, No. 1, August 15, 1956
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Black ink on white newsprint (6 pages)

From donor Jim Collison: 
“My involvement with the Waterloo Star was as a columnist.  I wrote the Smitty’s Punches column under the pen name Wellington Smith, because at the time I was employed as a staff writer for the Waterloo Courier.  If the editor or the publisher had known of my involvement with and advocacy of the “Negro cause” I would have been fired immediately.  The first column (in the September 13, 1956, issue) carried this Editor’s note: “The author, who wishes to remain anonymous, will use as his by-line names of two Negro friends he once worked with – Smith, respected for his honesty and courage; Wellington, equally courageous and honest, remembered for his friendly cheerfulness.
It seems the Waterloo Star ceased publication with the 12th issue, certainly sometime in early 1957.”
subject: African Americans; Black people; African American newspapers; Black newspapers

title: Waterloo Progress, Volume 1, No. 2, May 22, 1958
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Black ink on white newsprint (4 pages)

From donor Jim Collison:
“I started publishing the Waterloo Progress with the May 15, 1958, issue.  (I do not have a copy of the first issue.)  The goal was to treat Negroes and Negro news fairly in a local newspaper serving the Waterloo community.  I continued the Smitty’s Punches column in the Waterloo Progress.
I ceased publishing this newspaper after the fifth issue because the Waterloo Courier had spread the word to advertisers not to advertise in the Waterloo Progress.  My memory is that the Courier started offering advertisers cut rates for not advertising in my new paper.  When Russ Lowe (Advertising Director) told me this, I knew we had no chance to make a go of it.”

title: Greg “Streetz” Walker’s Referee Jersey
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
V-necked short-sleeve referee’s jersey in black and white striped polyester.  Machine-stitched.   
  
Greg Kent “Streetz” Walker, Jr. of Waterloo was a basketball referee for the Iowa Referee Association and respected for his work in local youth outreach.  He was tragically murdered on May 19, 2018.  His murder remains unsolved.
If you have information regarding the murder of Greg Walker, call the Waterloo Police Department at (319) 291-4383.

collectionid: Grout Museum District 2020 George Floyd Protests
title: Sean Berg Protest Sign from 2020 George Floyd Protests
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Wood cut print in black ink on white cardstock. Image at anterior depicts a prisoner holding a protest sign. “IOWA RECIDIVISM RATE IS CURRENTLY ALMOST 40% in Fiscal 2018" handwritten in red felt-tip marker. "BLACK LIVES MATTER" handwritten in blue and black felt-tip marker at posterior.

Used at the May 29 march from Veterans Memorial Hall to Lincoln Park in Waterloo and at the June 6 “Silent Vigil in Solidarity with BLM” held in Overman Park in Cedar Falls.

Artist’s statement:  
"I handed out, between C.F. Vigil for Floyd and Waterloo march, roughly 150 posters.  Each poster is a woodcut print, made by myself, w/ a different handwritten message on each print to help inform the public about racial disparity/inequality in Iowa/America.  I was only able to track down 21 of the originals, as I handed out every print free to strangers and other people I do not have direct contact with at all.  The signs/prints (original art on the one side w/ BLACK LIVES MATTER wrote [sic] on the reverse side) were held by marchers/protestors, so they have come full circle from myself (the artist) to the people (protestors/citizens) then back to me, and lastly to you all at the Grout Museum for archival and historical purposes . . . very cool."
subject: African Americans; Black people; Black lives matter movement; Protest movements; African Americans--Violence against; Police brutality

collectionid: Grout Museum District 2020 George Floyd Protests
title: Vikki Brown’s T-Shirt from 2020 George Floyd Protests
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Crew-necked short-sleeve t-shirt of white cotton. Machine-stitched. “WE WILL BE HEARD” printed in black at upper center anterior.   

Worn by Black Hawk County Democrats Chairwoman Vikki Brown during the May 29, 2020 George Floyd Protest in Waterloo. The May 29 protest began with a march from Veterans Memorial Hall to Lincoln Park and concluded with speeches made in the park. 
Brown grew up in Birmingham, Alabama and participated in the "Children's Crusade" (May 2-3, 1963) when she was 11 years old.  She was personally acquainted with one of the victims of the 16th Street Baptist Church Bombing (September 15, 1963).

From donor Vikki Brown:

“The civil rights movement in the south was very unstable during the Jim Crow era of the 1950s and 1960s; and at times it was also violent. Growing up in Birmingham, Alabama during that era as a little girl, I felt compelled to participate on the right side of history as a Child Crusader. Fearing for our safety, my mother wouldn’t allow me nor my sisters to go near the chaos, but in 1963 at the age of 11 I sneaked down with classmates and marched against injustice. The ugliness hit close to home when a neighborhood acquaintance, also 11 years old, lost her life in the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing in September of that same year. Today, 57 years later I still continue with a vision on the right side of history. I have always, and I will continue to have a personal commitment to making a positive difference and impact in my community far and wide.”
subject: African Americans; Black people; Black lives matter movement; Protest movements; African Americans--Violence against; Police brutality

- August 31, 2020, Hawkeye Community College, Waterloo.

collectionid: Grout Museum District 2020 George Floyd Protests
title: Joyce Levingston’s T-Shirt from 2020 George Floyd Protests
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Crew-necked short-sleeve t-shirt of black cotton. Machine-stitched. “BLACK LIVES MATTER” in white over a stylized fist printed in white at center anterior.   

Worn by Organizer Joyce Levingston during the 2020 George Floyd Protests in Waterloo, Cedar Falls, and the surrounding area.  Levingston herself organized (with others) the May 29 march from Veterans Memorial Hall to Lincoln Park in Waterloo.  Waterloo Courier photos depict Levingston in the t-shirt at the June 6 vigil at Overman Park, Cedar Falls.  T-shirt also worn at the June 20 march from Clay St. to Overman Park in Cedar Falls.

From donor Joyce Levingston:

“When I heard George Floyd call out to his momma knowing he was going to lose his life, my heart shattered. It was at that moment that I knew in my lifetime I had seen this happen too much to my own. After hearing about Breonna Taylor and seeing what happened to George Floyd and many others in my lifetime I looked at my three daughters and my son and decided we need to come together in our community for all of our voices to be heard and mourn together for once instead of us carrying the weight on our own.  Rest in peace Breonna Taylor and rest in peace George Floyd.”
subject: African Americans; Black people; Black lives matter movement; Protest movements; African Americans--Violence against; Police brutality

collectionid: Grout Museum District 2020 George Floyd Protests
title: Maggie Dvorak’s Covid Mask from 2020 George Floyd Protests
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Covid mask of black fabric with orange fabric backing. Hand-made. Machine-stitched. “END RACISM NOW” in white acrylic paint at center anterior.   

Mask made by Maggie Dvorak of Cedar Falls. Worn by donor at the June 6 vigil in Overman Park, Cedar Falls, and the march from Clay Street to Overman Park, June 20, Cedar Falls.  Waterloo Courier photos show donor in mask at the June 6 vigil.

From donor Maggie Dvorak:

“I chose to contribute masks for several events in the Cedar Valley. These were events to bring community leaders and members together to show support for Black lives mattering, and to denounce systemic racism in our communities, and world wide.

Due to Covid 19, and my belief in the importance of doing our best to protect each other during a world wide pandemic, it was important to provide as many masks as necessary for participants to have available for use.

I hope to continue to donate and contribute my voice and my mask making abilities in any opportunity I am able. I will do this in hopes of supporting community health, and to support bringing an end to the systemic racism present in our world.”

title: Lily Furgerson Doll
rightsstatement: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
description:
Hand-knit doll of black, blue, and white yarn.  14” tall.   
  
Made by Mrs. Lily Furgerson of Waterloo in 1944 and presented that year to 8-year-old Diane Broessel, also of Waterloo.  Furgerson was married to Dr. Lee B. Furgerson, MD, Waterloo’s second black physician.  He began his practice in Waterloo in 1927.  His wife made dolls for his child patients and this one was given to Broessel, who was suffering from rheumatic fever.  The Broessel family were among Dr. Furgerson’s white patients.  
After Dr. Furgerson’s death in 1948, Mrs. Furgerson pursued an education degree and became Waterloo’s first black schoolteacher in 1952.  She taught fourth through sixth grade for 19 years at Grant Elementary.
subject: African Americans; Black people; Art; African American children; Child care

title: Signature Quilt for St. Paul African Methodist Episcopal Church, Des Moines, 1940-1941
rightsstatement: No Known Copyright
corporate_contributor: St. Paul A.M.E. Church (Des Moines, Iowa)
description:
Quilt, synthetic fabric, three inch squares of pink and orange alternating with blocks of black; quilted with pink thread; backed with pink synthetic fabric, bound with black. Embroidered with names, date, and name of St. Paul AME Church. Circa 1940-1941. 62 x 71.5 inches.

Handmade by donor's grandmother, Nora McKee Dixon, 1870-1958, Des Moines, Iowa, Polk County, as fundraiser for St. Paul African Methodist Episcopal Church of Des Moines at the beginning of World War II. Nora's daughter, Madeline Dixon Berry, did the handwriting on the blocks. Names of church officials, including bishop, pastor, elders, embroidered on middle orange and black blocks. Names around the center represent those who gave ten cents to the Junior Missionaries to have their names on the quilt.

Photographs of the McKee family including Nora as a girl and young woman are held in Special Collections, Des Moines. The family of Madaline and John McKee were residents of Dallas County after the Civil War.

title: Poster, Roll of Honor, World War I
rightsstatement: No Known Copyright
description:
Roster of 150 African American men who served during World War I at Camp Dodge and other locations including Camp Grant, Snowfall Barracks, Newport News, Viginia, Des Moines, Hawaiian Islands, and Somewhere in France. Handwritten text in columns, drawing of two soldiers on either side of red and white striped banner. Drawn & arr'ng by Roy Burns. About 1918. 21.75 x 15.5 inches. Presented to the museum by the Colored Women's Club of Des Moines.
